data.title: IBM, Lam Research Focus High-NA EUV on Sub-1-nm Nodes
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

IBM and Lam Research aim to make chips beyond the 1-nm node. The companies, however, declined to disclose potential chipmaking partners or a timeline for commercial production.

IBM and Lam Research have a five-year agreement focused on developing new materials, fabrication processes, and High-NA EUV lithography to advance IBM’s logic scaling roadmap, the companies said in a March 10th news release. In 2021, IBM became the world’s first to make a 2-nm chip, and later partnered with Japan’s Rapidus to manufacture 2-nm devices by next year.

IBM and Lam Research aim to develop High-NA EUV lithography processes for new interconnect and device patterning, thereby accelerating industry adoption of ASML’s most advanced lithography tool. Foundries like industry leader TSMC haven’t adopted High-NA EUV yet and instead continue to use multi-patterning workarounds that extend the use of Low-NA EUV patterning tools.

“We are thrilled to be expanding our collaboration to tackle the next set of challenges to enable High-NA EUV lithography and sub-1-nm nodes,” Mukesh Khare, general manager of IBM Semiconductors at IBM Research, stated in the press release.

In the new era of 3D scaling, progress relies on materials, processes, and lithography that come together as a single, high-density system, said Vahid Vahedi, chief technology and sustainability officer at Lam Research.

“We are proud to build on our successful collaboration with IBM to drive High-NA EUV dry resist and process breakthroughs, accelerating the development of lower-power and higher-performance transistors that will be critical for the AI era,” Vahedi stated.

IBM earlier this year said it aims for sub-2 nm nodes with heat-modeling technology it developed with Synopsys and the support of U.S. military research agency DARPA. The companies told EE Times they will share the technology with chipmaking partners as the 2-nm node ramps up. TSMC and other chipmakers, such as Samsung, started industry-leading 2-nm node manufacturing last year.

Stumbling block

The 2-nm node that’s critical for the advance of AI has been a stumbling block for some of the world’s leading chipmakers, including Samsung and Intel. TSMC dominates the most advanced node that’s in commercial production, and the company has done it without adopting High-NA EUV.

Defining a decade

IBM expects High-NA EUV to define the next decade of chip scaling.

“IBM believes accelerating its adoption will unlock the high-performance AI chips needed for the AI era,” Khare told EE Times. “However, reaching that point will require advances in materials, etch and deposition processes, pattern transfer, and more to capture the cost and performance benefits.”

As logic devices move toward sub-1 nm, pattern transfer and defectivity have become fundamental barriers, Vahedi told EE Times.

“Device dimensions push the limits of physics, requiring entirely new materials, patterning techniques, and atomic-level process control strategies,” Vahedi said. “Yield is increasingly driven by atomic-scale fluctuations and process interactions. This makes co-optimized, atomic-scale deposition and etch flows a key challenge for sub-1-nm logic. Lam will focus on enabling atomic‑level precision in patterning, etch, and deposition so that ultra-fine EUV patterns can be reliably transferred into real device layers with production‑level yield.”

Scaling to advanced nodes increasingly requires tighter pitches than can be achieved with Low-NA single patterning, Lam said.

“In Low-NA, these advanced nodes require multiple patterning, which results in higher cost, longer cycle times, and lower yield,” Vahedi said.

Lam Research’s latest dry-resist and patterning technologies improve resolution and defectivity, enabling direct-print High-NA EUV patterning—giving chipmakers improved yield with better patterning resolution, he added.

IBM’s chip legacy

After exiting the chip manufacturing business decades ago, IBM has continued to develop semiconductor technology at the Albany NanoTech Complex in the state of New York. The New York facility hosts the first public-private High-NA EUV lithography center in North America. In addition to its work with Rapidus at Albany NanoTech, IBM has a foundry partnership with Samsung.

In this latest semiconductor partnership with Lam Research, “our teams aim to develop full process flows for nanosheet and nanostack devices and backside power delivery,” Khare said. “The goal is to enable reliable transfer of High-NA EUV patterns into device layers with high yield and support continued scaling, improved performance, and viable paths to production for future logic devices.”

See also:

Lam Research, CEA-Leti Open Fast Lane for Specialty Technologies

IBM, Synopsys Move Toward 1.4-nm Node With Heat-Modeling Tech

The annual embedded world 2026 show took place this week (March 10-12) in Nuremberg, Germany, with some 36,000 visitors attending from almost 90 countries. As the leading global gathering of people involved in the entire electronics and embedded systems value chain, the event is a showcase of the latest technologies enabling what the industry is calling physical AI, which was otherwise also known as edge AI, and robotics.

In this video conversation, Nitin Dahad, executive editor of EE Times, and Jim McGregor, principal analyst at TIRIAS Research, discuss their key observations from talking to many executives and developers at the three-day trade fair, plus some of the trends and highlights.

data.title: MWC 2026 Concludes as Telcos Pivot to AI
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The Mobile World Congress (MWC) 2026 closed last week, underscoring the telecom industry’s pivotal shift from legacy hardware to AI-driven infrastructure.

Marking its 20th year in Barcelona, the event drew 105,000 attendees from 207 nations, reinforcing its status as the leading global connectivity gathering despite ongoing travel disruptions in the Middle East.

The GSM Association (GSMA), the organizing body, designated this year’s theme as “The IQ Era.” The theme reflects a departure from the industry’s historical focus on bandwidth expansion, shifting attention to dynamically programmable networks and the impending 6G standard.

In an opening address, GSMA director general Vivek Badrinath articulated the sector’s current mandate. “If we want to unlock the full promise of 5G, harness AI responsibly, and protect people from escalating digital threats, we must act with urgency,” Badrinath stated.

John Hoffman, CEO of GSMA, reflected on the location’s role over the past two decades, noting in a statement that “Barcelona had the infrastructure, the ecosystem and the commitment to grow with us.”

Hardware infrastructure divide

Reporting from the exhibition floor, EE Times detailed a dividing line regarding the physical foundations for AI and next-generation networks.

Telecommunications operators are debating whether to process AI workloads using distributed graphics processing units (GPUs) or through central processing units (CPUs) that feature integrated AI acceleration. This architectural choice carries significant implications for capital expenditures, power grid stress, and equipment deployment at the network edge.

Nvidia and Nokia advance distributed GPUs

Nvidia has aggressively pursued the telecommunications market, advocating for GPUs as the standard for radio access network (RAN) operations. The company presented its Aerial RAN Computer (ARC), promoting the network as a computational platform that concurrently handles standard telecommunications functions alongside external AI applications.

Ronnie Vasishta, SVP of telecom at Nvidia, told EE Times that the company views cellular access points as the future connectivity fabric for AI inference. Vasishta argued that operators must move away from fixed hardware systems. “It’s like you don’t wait for a TV because the next model is better… to watch TV,” Vasishta said. “Buy something that can be software upgradable, and that’s what the AI native platform is.” To support this, Nvidia released an open-source telecommunications model based on its Nemotron 3 architecture to manage network fault detection.

Nokia aligned its strategy with Nvidia, supported by a $1 billion investment. CEO Justin Hotard argued legacy silicon no longer suffices.

“AI is the new workload reshaping networks. That shift requires architectural change across every layer, including the radio,” Hotard said during a pre-MWC media event. By adopting the computing architecture used in data centers, Nokia aims to separate hardware from software to process complex workloads at the radio edge.

Ericsson and Arm target CPU efficiency

In contrast, Ericsson, Intel, and Arm maintain that CPU-based architecture is more practical for telecommunications. This contingent asserts that deploying GPUs for edge inference adds unwarranted complexity and risks overloading the utility power limits of cell sites.

Arm positioned its Neoverse computing platform as a power-efficient alternative for operators integrating AI. Eddie Ramírez, VP of Arm’s infrastructure business, told EE Times that energy limitations strictly dictate facility capacity. “When you build a data center, the amount of compute is dictated by power,” Ramírez stated. He observed that operators are pivoting from GPU rental to “inference as a service,” seeking air-cooled hardware for edge networks.

Ramírez also noted that autonomous AI agents will fundamentally alter data patterns. “What is going to change is when it’s not you making the queries, it’s an AI agent,” he explained, warning operators to prepare for sustained data traffic.

Ericsson maintains software independence by utilizing purpose-built silicon, a decision intended to avoid vendor lock-in with GPU providers. In an EE Times interview, Marie Hogan, head of 6G portfolio strategy at Ericsson, described a mixed approach to computing distribution.

“Some models that require massive compute will likely be centralized if they don’t have strict timing pressure. Smaller models and compute may be localized and integrated into radio or base station hardware,” Hogan said.

Ericsson’s CTO, Erik Ekudden, emphasized the need for immediate deployment. “There is no need to wait for 6G when it comes to AI. AI is here, both in the network and as a business opportunity,” he noted.

Qualcomm focuses on network sensing

During MWC 2026, Qualcomm outlined a 6G vision that expands the network into an environmental sensing system capable of spatial mapping. By integrating sensing and communications capabilities, the network can transmit information while operating as a radar system to detect objects.

Cristiano Amon, president and CEO of Qualcomm, described the long-term impact of this technology. “6G is more than the next step in wireless evolution. It is the foundation for an AI-native future that distributes intelligence across devices, the edge, and the cloud, and transforms network providers into AI-driven enterprises,” Amon stated.

John Smee, Qualcomm’s global head of wireless engineering, cautioned against relying solely on centralized computing in a briefing with EE Times. “We think it’s very important that there’s a holistic approach to energy consumption and to be part of the value creation,” Smee said.

Qualcomm proposes distributing AI tasks across wearables, the network edge, and data centers to extend battery life for consumer devices such as smart glasses.

Robotics, physical AI, and monetization

During the show, companies conducted live demonstrations of robotics and physical AI. Nokia and Nvidia demonstrated this capability by operating a robotic dog via an AI-powered 5G connection with Indosat Ooredoo Hutchison in Indonesia.

Operators are aggressively targeting robotics as a new profit engine for current infrastructure. T-Mobile US unveiled ‘Kinetic Tokens’ to convert physical AI movements directly into revenue.

The GSMA also reported commercial progress on its Open Gateway initiative, a series of application programming interfaces that enable enterprise developers to set network performance parameters.

Providers now use these interfaces to secure drones and drive industrial automation, leaving behind routine consumer broadband. A European operator consortium also announced the ‘Edge Continuum’—a federated edge computing network for autonomous vehicles that cross borders.

Preparing for the 6G transition

The technical debates at the 2026 Mobile World Congress established the foundation for 6G standardizations, which the 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) is currently formalizing for an expected commercial release in 2029.

Hardware executives insist the shift to software-defined networks must precede the standard’s completion to meet current data needs.

As utility costs climb, the future of telecom hinges on executing AI within strict energy bounds. Strategies revealed in Barcelona will define the next decade of network economics and connectivity.

​See also:

Nvidia Advances AI-Native Strategy at MWC

At MWC, Intel Outlines CPU-Driven Case for Telecom Networks

Nokia Bets the Network on Nvidia in AI and 6G Pivot

At MWC, Qualcomm Outlines AI-Native 6G Vision

data.title: The Silicon Hegemon
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

In today’s global economy, one key link holds everything together. Surprisingly, it doesn’t lead to Silicon Valley, Shenzhen, or Munich but to a subtropical island that was once known for sugar and rice exports and is now a global leader in semiconductor manufacturing.

Today, Taiwan does not merely participate in the global technology supply chain; it sets the pace. As of early 2026, the island produces approximately 92% of the world’s most advanced logic chips—those measuring 5 nm and smaller.

These components serve as the brains for everything from the AI algorithms reshaping human labor to the guidance systems of hypersonic missiles. This concentration of industrial power has created a paradox. Taiwan is both the world’s most essential economy and its most fragile single point of failure.

At the center of this hegemony stands Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), a corporate titan that has effectively decoupled from the cyclical downturns of consumer electronics to ride the AI supercycle. Yet as TSMC pushes the boundaries of physics with its new 2-nm process, it faces a collision course with the finite limits of Taiwan’s energy grid and the volatility of “great power” politics.

Foundry revolution

To understand why Taiwan leads today, it helps to look back at a big risk taken almost 40 years ago. In the mid-1980s, companies known as integrated device manufacturers, such as Intel and Texas Instruments, both designed and manufactured their own chips.

Morris Chang, a veteran of Texas Instruments who had been passed over for the CEO role, was recruited by the Taiwanese government to modernize the island’s primitive electronics sector. Backed by government minister K.T. Li, TSMC was founded in 1987 with a radical premise: the “pure-play foundry” model.

Chang saw that while there were talented chip designers around the world, few had the money needed to build factories, or “fabs.” By making a company that only manufactured chips for others and didn’t compete in design, TSMC brought together demand from across the industry. This approach helped companies such as Nvidia, Apple, and Qualcomm grow by letting them focus on design and outsource production to Taiwan.

The foundation for this success was laid even earlier with the 1976 RCA Project, a technology transfer deal between Taiwan’s Industrial Technology Research Institute and the Radio Corporation of America. In fact, Taiwan became a top chip manufacturer with help from the United States.

The initiative, along with the early assembly operations established by Texas Instruments, trained a generation of Taiwanese engineers and the famed “transistor girls”—assembly-line workers renowned for their manual dexterity and discipline. This historical transfer of know-how from the U.S. has seeded the precision-driven industrial culture that defines Taiwan today.

Critical node

Today, that early risk has turned into a near-monopoly. Taiwan accounts for about 20% of the world’s semiconductor output, but its control over the most advanced chips is complete. In the market for cutting-edge chips, TSMC holds over 90%, while competitors such as Samsung and Intel struggle to gain ground.

The scale of this dominance is huge. Taiwan’s semiconductor production is expected to reach $197.2 billion in 2025, driven by strong demand for AI accelerators.

In just the fourth quarter of 2025, TSMC reported a gross margin of 62.3%, which is unusually high for a capital-intensive business and demonstrates its strong pricing power. The company used 305 process technologies, made 12,682 products for 534 customers, and expects to earn US$35 billion in revenue and a 64% gross margin in the first quarter of 2026. These numbers include both Taiwan and overseas operations.

TSMC Chairman and CEO C.C. Wei told investors in late 2025 that he was not worried about an AI bubble. The demand from AI continues to be strong, Wei said, adding that he had “not observed any change in our customers’ behavior so far” when it comes to spending.

This strong demand has allowed TSMC to separate its financial results from slower sectors such as smartphones and cars, enabling it to capture most of the industry’s profits.

The gap between TSMC and its competitors has widened significantly. Samsung, the only other company trying to compete at the 3-nm and 2-nm level, has struggled with production problems. In late 2025, Samsung’s foundry leaders admitted to employees that their technology “falls behind other big manufacturers.”

At the same time, Intel’s attempt to catch up with its 18A node has come under scrutiny. Wei said that TSMC’s current 3-nm technology matches Intel’s future 18A and that TSMC’s upcoming 2-nm technology will be “more advanced than both.”

TSMC PROCESS TECHNOLOGY ROADMAP (2024–2028)

The Angstrom era

Keeping this lead means mastering the science of the extremely small. As the industry enters the Angstrom era, in which sizes are measured in tenths of a nanometer, TSMC is making one of the most difficult technical changes in its history. Its special partnership with ASML, the Dutch company that makes the lithography machines critical to make those chips, and its unique expertise keep TSMC ahead of competitors.

“Taiwan’s pure-play foundry model has built a global foundation for semiconductor design companies,” Mateo Valero, director of the Barcelona Supercomputing Center, said. “In the Angstrom era, with technologies such as GAA [gate-all-around], Super Power Rail, and CoWoS [chip-on-wafer-on-substrate] packaging, Taiwan remains a key player to the AI revolution. TSMC’s advancements in Moore’s Law drive progress from high-performance computing to efficient devices, making Taiwan a key technical hub of today’s digital economy.”

In the last quarter of 2025, TSMC started large-scale production of its 2-nm chips. This new technology ends the use of the FinFET transistor design, which has been the standard for 10 years. Instead, TSMC is using nanosheet transistors, also called gate-all-around. These wrap the gate material all the way around the silicon channel, which stops electrical leaks and boosts performance by 10% to 15% or cuts power use by up to 30% compared with the previous generation.

But TSMC’s plans go further: The company has announced its A16 node, set for the second half of 2026, which will use Super Power Rail technology. Normally, power and data wires compete for space on the front of the silicon wafer, leading to congestion.

With A16, TSMC will send power through the back of the wafer using new materials such as ruthenium. This change fixes critical wiring issues in high-performance computing and competes directly with Intel’s PowerVia solution.

Even more important than the transistors are how they are packaged. For modern AI systems, the main limit is not just how fast they process but how quickly they can move data between memory and logic.

TSMC’s CoWoS packaging is now the most limited resource in the global supply chain. CoWoS lets Nvidia’s GPUs and high-bandwidth memory stacks sit side by side on a silicon base, creating a large data highway.

“At TSMC, a higher level of capital expenditures is always correlated with higher growth opportunities,” CFO Wendell Huang said, explaining why the company will raise capital spending to between US$50 billion and US$55 billion in 2026. Much of this money will go to expanding advanced packaging, which should almost double to 1.275 million wafers in 2026 to meet the needs of its Grand Alliance of customers.

Grand Alliance and local supply chains

TSMC does not work alone; it is at the center of what it calls the Grand Alliance, a group of intellectual-property providers, equipment makers, and material suppliers. At a ceremony in Arizona, Wei described this network as a partnership meant to “unleash innovation.”

In the past, Taiwan depended on Japan and the U.S. for important materials and equipment. But due to geopolitical risks, the supply chain is becoming more localized. Taiwanese company GlobalWafers is now the world’s third-largest supplier of silicon substrates. Companies like LCY Chemical also provide extremely pure isopropyl alcohol, which is needed to clean wafers at the 3-nm level.

This close cooperation makes it hard for competitors to enter the market. Partners in the Open Innovation Platform get early access to TSMC’s process design kits, which tie their chip designs to TSMC’s manufacturing methods. Switching a design from TSMC to another foundry is not easy—it requires a major, costly redesign, creating a strong moat around Taiwan’s foundry business.

Green Paradox

The constant push for smaller, better chips has run into Taiwan’s physical limits. Producing semiconductors consumes significant resources, creating a “Green Paradox”: Making energy-efficient chips now takes more and more energy.

TSMC alone consumes approximately 9% of Taiwan’s total electricity generation, a figure projected to rise to 12% by 2030. The transition to High-NA EUV lithography, the next generation of tools required for Angstrom-level manufacturing, exacerbates this challenge. A single High-NA machine consumes up to 1.4 MW of power, roughly 20% more than current models. This demand places immense stress on Taiwan’s power grid, which has struggled with stability following the phase-out of nuclear power and delays in renewable energy projects.

Water shortages are another serious problem. Climate change has altered Taiwan’s typhoon patterns, leading to severe droughts in recent years. In response, TSMC has built some of the world’s most advanced water-recycling systems. By 2024, the company replaced 17% of its total water use with reclaimed industrial wastewater, treated to be even purer than drinking water.

There is also a shortage of skilled workers. Taiwan has one of the world’s lowest birth rates, leading to a shortage of talent. In May 2025, the semiconductor industry was short about 34,000 workers. To fix this, the government has set up special semiconductor colleges, and companies are using more automation and hiring foreign workers to fill the gap.

End of the ‘Silicon Shield’

Every chip made in Hsinchu is affected by global politics. The “silicon shield” theory says that Taiwan’s key role in the world economy helps protect it from military threats by China. The idea is that because China imports more semiconductors than oil, destroying Taiwan’s chip factories would hurt China’s own economy.

“Our semiconductor industry is especially significant: a ‘silicon shield’ that allows Taiwan to protect itself and others from aggressive attempts by authoritarian regimes to disrupt global supply chains,” Tsai Ing-wen, then-president of Taiwan, said in an article in Foreign Affairs in November 2021.

But this theory is now being tested. The U.S. is using export controls to limit China’s access to advanced chips and equipment, while Beijing is investing billions in its Big Fund to become self-sufficient in older chip technologies. Some experts say that if China feels permanently blocked from advanced technology, the “silicon shield” may lose its deterrence power.

Furthermore, recent news reports of China developing an advanced EUV lithography machine, something that only ASML produces, could allow Chinese companies to make advanced node processors without relying on foreign technology.

The economic risks of a conflict are almost impossible to measure. Some estimates say a war over Taiwan could erase $10 trillion, or about 10% of global GDP, which would be much worse than the 2008 financial crisis or the COVID-19 pandemic. According to the “broken nest” theory discussed by U.S. defense experts, Taiwan’s chip factories might be shut down in the event of an invasion to prevent them from being taken over. This scorched-earth approach could send the global tech industry into a depression.

To deal with these risks, TSMC has started a strategy of globalization by necessity. The company is spending $65 billion to build three factories in Arizona, with the first one set to start 4-nm production. TSMC has also expanded into Japan and Germany to support the car and industrial markets.

However, TSMC leaders have made it clear that this regional expansion has limits. The most advanced research and the largest production of cutting-edge chips will stay in Taiwan. The Arizona factories are meant to be one generation behind those in Taiwan, so the most advanced technologies, such as A14 and the latest CoWoS packaging, remain in Hsinchu and Kaohsiung.

As 2026 begins, Taiwan remains the semiconductor industry’s stronghold. Its dominance shows the impact of Morris Chang’s vision and the strength of the Grand Alliance. However, for the rest of the world, this creates an uneasy sense of dependence.

The global economy has put its digital future in the hands of one island, hoping that careful diplomacy and deterrence will keep the factories running. For now, the chips keep coming, but this requires constant attention and caution.

data.title: Beyond Bandwidth: The Industry is Striving for Custom Memory (Part 2)
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

In Part 1 of this series, we discussed HBM4 memory, which is set to deliver unprecedented bandwidth due to its 2,048-bit interface and high data transfer rates. But for applications that need even more bandwidth and capacity, the industry is prepping multiple types of custom memory. In Part 2, we cover what Global Unichip Corp. (GUC), Marvell, Samsung, and imec have to offer when it comes to custom memory solutions, including high-bandwidth memory (HBM)-on-top-of-logic.

Marvell custom DRAM

Marvell was among the first developers of custom processors—the company calls them XPUs to emphasize that they are accelerators, not GPUs—to talk about its approach to custom HBM4E memory, and there are good chances that Marvell-designed XPU platforms will be among the first to adopt custom HBM4E DRAM produced by Micron, Samsung, or SK Hynix.

Marvell’s custom DRAM architecture for AI accelerators replaces the traditional, area-hungry 2,048-bit HBM4 PHY with Marvell’s proprietary 32-GT/s 512-bit bidirectional die-to-die (D2D) interface that operates at up to a 32-GT/s data transfer rate and relocates the memory controller into a custom base die beneath the DRAM stack. The DRAM devices in the stack remain JEDEC-standard (i.e., HBM4E), and the whole stack also maintains JEDEC-set geometry specifications, so Marvell’s clients do not have to worry about using proprietary memory devices (which are expensive) or memory stacks with non-standard z-heights.

“We want to leverage all of the work already done by JEDEC on DRAM stacking,” Khurram Malik, senior director of product marketing for CXL, custom HBM, memory, and storage products at Marvell Technology told EE Times. “From the DRAM stack perspective, it remains JEDEC-compliant. The DRAM [device] stack is standard HBM4E. The customization happens in the base die and in the interface to the compute die.”

“[DRAM devices, production methods, electrical specs, and dimensions] remain governed by JEDEC standards even when a custom interface is used,” Nidish Kamath, director of product management for silicon IP at Rambus, told EE Times. “The DRAM layers, physical dimensions, and electrical characteristics are standardized to ensure operability and reliability. What changes is the way the base die communicates with the host, which could be either proprietary [e.g., with a bunch-of-wires implementation] or standards-based [e.g., using a packetized format over UCIe]. This separation helps maintain supply chain stability while enabling differentiation at the logic level.”

The removal of the standard 2,048-bit HBM4E PHY from the compute die and replacing it with Marvell’s proprietary 32-GT/s bidirectional D2D interface frees up to 25% of SoC real estate for compute hardware, cuts memory I/O power consumption by 45% to 70% depending on scenarios, increases the amount of memory supported by the SoC by 33%, or reduces the SoC cost (by cutting down die size) compared with standard HBM implementations. In addition to these benefits, Marvell’s custom HBM4E DRAM architecture also provides higher peak bandwidth than JEDEC’s standard HBM4E: One HBM4E stack (12 GT/s, 2,048 bits) provides up to 3.072 TB/s of bandwidth, whereas one Marvell custom HBM4E stack (32 GT/s, 512 bits) offers 2.048 TB/s, so four stacks not only provide 4× capacity but also 2.66× higher bandwidth (8.192 TB/s) over the same 2,048-bit interface.

At the physical layer, Marvell’s D2D link runs at 64 GT/s per wire bidirectionally (32 GT/s each way), offering over 30 Tb/s/mm of bandwidth density (when implemented on 2-nm- or 3-nm-class nodes), which is considerably higher than what current-generation UCIe links can achieve. The IP integrates lane redundancy with automatic repair, ECC, and RAS capabilities, as well as adaptive power control that tracks bursty traffic. Yet Marvell says little about its proprietary interface for competitive reasons.

“The solution includes enhanced ECC, advanced RAS telemetry, and reliability engines,” Malik said. “We do not publicly disclose the exact signaling or coding methods, but these features are essential for operating at such high speeds.”

Marvell delivers its custom HBM4E memory as a turnkey solution that includes application bridge, link layers, and physical interconnect implemented using 2-nm- and 3-nm-class process technologies. The company also stresses that its custom HBM4E memory can also integrate additional capabilities into the custom base dies, thus bringing acceleration even closer to memory.

“If hyperscalers would like to bring some compute capabilities or some logic into the base die for acceleration, there has to be customization,” Malik said. “[We cannot disclose active projects.] … What is happening is wherever there is more capacity, customers are looking at how to bring acceleration closer to that.”

Malik declined to disclose how many of Marvell’s customers are developing XPUs with the proprietary D2D interface or with custom HBM4E memory that has certain accelerators in their base die, though it is safe to assume the number is higher than zero.

Global Unichip Corp. DRAM-on-Logic

GUC’s DRAM-on-Logic (DoL) is arguably the most exotic type of memory among those discussed in this article. GUC’s DoL comes from a contract chip designer, it has a very particular positioning, and it relies on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s (TSMC’s) SoIC hybrid bonding.

Although GUC formally places DoL between on-die SRAM and off-package HBM in the memory hierarchy, a closer look at the performance, power, and cost data suggests that DoL is not primarily designed to coexist with HBM or DDR in hybrid memory subsystems (which are beyond the scope of this article). Instead, DoL seems to be a near-compute working memory tier that is faster than HBM4 and can replace it in applications that benefit from bandwidth and do not require high capacity.

In GUC’s implementation, four to eight customized DRAM layers are vertically bonded directly on top of the compute die, which eliminates industry-standard external memory interfaces, PHYs, and SerDes, freeing up space for compute hardware. This structure delivers up to about 5 TB/s of bandwidth (beating HBM4 by double) at an approximately 30-ns latency, at about a 0.5-pJ/bit energy efficiency, according to numbers shared by GUC at a TSMC forum. The power efficiency of GUC’s DoL is about 0.5 pJ/bit, versus 3 pJ/bit for HBM4 and 0.1 pJ/bit for SRAM, according to the company.

As for density, GUC says DoL offers a memory density of 10 MB/mm2 to 40 MB/mm2 depending on stack height, therefore delivering tens of gigabytes per device, which is sufficient for many AI inference and networking workloads that are constrained more by bandwidth and power than by raw memory capacity. For these applications, the architectural and economic rationale of HBM4 makes far less sense than GUC’s DoL. HBM4 provides considerably more capacity but at a higher latency, lower bandwidth, and higher cost. Because HBM4 delivers significantly higher density, it will remain indispensable for advanced model training and ultra-large models that require hundreds of gigabytes of memory, but in a growing number of accelerators, DoL can act as the primary high-performance memory. Meanwhile, those who need additional capacity can build a hybrid memory subsystem containing both DoL and DDR/LPDDR memory, though it remains to be seen whether such systems will become widespread.

From a cost and integration perspective, GUC positions DoL at roughly “1× plus SoIC cost” per gigabit, which is compared with “2× plus CoWoS cost” for HBM4 and roughly 20× for SRAM. The stacked structure allows the memory to scale vertically without consuming a lot of die or interposer area as well as benefit from all power optimizations that its SoC may have.

Yet GUC’s DoL may come with some caveats. Because the memory needs to align perfectly with the logic, it will have to be manufactured using process technologies that eliminate even the slightest misalignment. This requires a careful choice of manufacturing nodes. In theory, this can all be achieved, but we do not know anything about yield at larger-scale production yet, and low yields mean high costs.

“DRAM stacked on logic would be ideal,” Michael Schuette, CTO of DataSecure as well as CTO and chief scientist of Boolean Labs, who has multiple patents in the field of memory, told EE Times. “But you are looking at two different manufacturing processes, and the smaller the geometry, the more difficult it gets to align the different blocks.”

Also, it is unclear whether it is possible to test DoL’s DRAM layers individually before assembling them together with logic. If it is impossible, one bad DRAM layer can kill the whole assembly, which would skyrocket costs and make the whole technology unpopular, something that happened to RDRAM in the late 1990s to early 2010s.

“Rambus’s RDRAM serial architecture did not allow testing of individual chips, only the entire module,” Schuette said. “If you had one bad row of memory, you had to throw away the entire module because there was no way to identify the bad chip.”

Samsung SAINT-D

GUC is not the only company proposing to stack DRAM on top of logic, as this approach provides benefits unattainable with traditional DRAM or HBM placement alongside processors. Samsung offers DoL integration as part of its Samsung Advanced Interconnection Technology (SAINT) 3D stacking platform, which includes SAINT-S (SRAM-on-logic), SAINT-L (logic-on-logic), and SAINT-D (DRAM-on-logic), with HBM/DRAM stacked vertically on CPU/GPU.

As the name suggests, SAINT-D is a DRAM stacking platform that provides numerous options for system-in-package developers seeking vertical memory integration. To that end, it supports integration of custom DRAM, HBM, or commodity DRAM, which makes it more versatile than GUC’s DoL, which is designed for very specific applications with very specific requirements. Samsung offers SAINT-D as part of its turnkey service, which combines its foundry business, advanced packaging offerings, and memory business. This one-stop-shop approach could be SAINT-D’s unique advantage: As Samsung produces all components internally, it can optimize all of them to deliver the best result, as vertical integration of HBM4 is expected to be particularly challenging.

Stacking HBM4 on top of logic

To improve performance, HBM4 widens its interface to 2,048 bits, which greatly increases the complexity of connecting it to host processors via an interposer and redistribution layer due to an extreme pin count of about 5,500. This is why it was widely believed that stacking HBM4 on top of host processors and connecting to them using hybrid bonding made more sense. However, HBM stacks are among the main sources of heat in AI accelerator packages, and the vertical stack massively increases local power density and thermal resistance, creating a severe heat bottleneck.

“Stacking HBM directly on top of processors has always been an intriguing idea, but practical challenges remain,” Steven Woo, fellow and distinguished inventor at Rambus, told EE Times. “Thermal management, power delivery, and yield issues make such integration difficult at scale, especially as both logic and memory densities increase. The industry continues to use side-by-side integration using advanced packaging such as 2.5D or chiplet-based approaches. These methods deliver high bandwidth without the thermal, power delivery, and manufacturing challenges introduced with 3D stacking.”

According to a paper published by imec in late 2025, without mitigation, a simulated 3D HBM-on-GPU solution—consisting of a GPU topped with four 12-Hi HBM stacks connected to the host via microbumps and cooled from above—hits 141.7°C peak GPU temperature, compared with 69.1°C for a conventional 2.5D HBM-around-GPU design under the same cooling conditions. This far exceeds the safe operating limits for both GPU logic (about 105°C) and DRAM (95°C). Such extreme temperatures, coupled with high power density, are not surprising. KAIST estimates that one 12-Hi/16-Hi HBM4 stack dissipates about 75 W while measuring 10.5 × 12.0 mm, and future versions of HBM will only increase their thermal design power. As a result, four 12-Hi HBM4 stacks would dissipate about 300 W under load, and when combined with a 400-W ASIC (such as one of two used by GB200/GB300), one ends up with 700 W of power produced by a 3D stacked processor that measures about 750 mm2 and is extremely hard to cool down.

“Now you have a logic die underneath that provides a nice heat source—how are you going to transport all that heat out and keep those memory contents with high integrity and in a reliable fashion?” Woo asked. “The DRAM stack does not really like being hot because the bit cells, which hold your data, will leak faster.”

To solve the thermal challenge posed by 3D stacked HBM, imec proposes severe mitigations under its cross-technology co-optimization (XTCO) program that combines system technology co-optimization and design technology co-optimization strategies. Among the things proposed are halving GPU frequency (−20.8°C), merging HBM stacks (−17.6°C), and double-sided cooling (−16.6°C), just to name the most efficient ones.

Taken together, XTCO steps can halve the temperature of a processor with 3D stacked HBM. However, this comes at a massive performance hit and requires HBM design change by DRAM makers, which could affect yields and the supply chain, as well as modifications to cooling methods.

“Halving the GPU core frequency brought the peak temperature from 120°C to below 100°C, achieving a key target for the memory operation,” James Myers, system technology program director at imec, said. “Although this step comes with a 28% workload penalty [i.e., a slowdown of AI training steps], the overall package outperforms the 2.5D baseline thanks to a higher throughput density offered by the 3D configuration. We are currently using this approach to study other GPU/HBM configurations [e.g., placing GPUs on top of HBMs], anticipating future thermal constraints.”

Whether actual developers of AI or HPC processors decide to 3D stack HBM4, HBM4E, or C-HBM4E on top of their designs is something that remains to be seen, but Rambus expects architectures influenced by thermals to be more common in the future.

“[Stacking memory on top of logic] is a challenge,” Kamath said. “It is going to be a bit of a tradeoff—how much work can you really do while still keeping DRAMs in a range that they are going to be reliable? But I think what is starting to happen … physical design constraints are influencing processor architectures. … Across the industry, we are seeing thermals and thermal effects influencing architectures, both on the processor and the memory side, and I think we are going to see more thermally influenced architecture decisions going forward.”

Memory meets logic: Automata, HBM-PIM, GDDR6-AIM

Incorporating custom logic into memory chips is not something completely new. Micron did this with its Automata Processor in 2014, whereas Samsung and SK Hynix experimented with this technology with their HBM-PIM (processor-in-memory) and GDDR6-AIM (accelerator-in-memory) in the early 2020s. There is a great rationale in putting basic processing capabilities near memory, as they both reduce requirements for memory bandwidth and ultimately power consumption.

“Ideally, you would want some kind of ‘in-memory-processing,’ a smart chip that is aware of the contents in the memory or at least has the capability to extract patterns,” Schuette said. “That way, you can reduce the overall bandwidth requirement to the host processor by, let’s say, 90%. … You can put in a processor that does pattern recognition, image analysis, whatever you want to do as a first pass, before you funnel the data out to the main processor.”

However, none of the three technologies took off, as all of them targeted niche workloads or misidentified bottlenecks.

Automata Processor embedded massive numbers of parallel finite-state machines in DRAM to accelerate pattern matching, regex, and graph-like traversal. However, it was effectively unusable outside of these domains and required specialized toolchains, compilers, and software developers acquainted with the automata theory.

While conceptually different, HBM-PIM and GDDR6-AIM were fundamentally designed to accomplish the same thing: Reduce data movement (an expensive task from a power consumption point of view) by integrating simple compute units into HBM stacks, thus boosting usable memory bandwidth. Samsung’s PIM integrated compute units into memory layers, whereas SK Hynix’s AIM integrated them into memory banks. However, neither concept has taken off, to a large degree because GPUs already offered adequate performance for many of the workloads envisioned for PIM and AIM and came with mature ecosystems (CUDA, cuDNN, PyTorch), which made a niche accelerator—even one offering large speedups on specific tasks—economically and operationally unattractive.

At the end of the day, AI workloads these days consolidate around dense tensor math, whereas Automata, PIM, and AIM addressed yesterday’s problems, such as irregular access, rules-based processing, or pattern matching.

Finally, memory makers such as Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix benefit more from selling standardized, high-margin HBM at scale. Their business models are not tailored for funding niche architectures with limited customer bases, while their fabrication technologies are better-suited for producing regular and repeatable DRAM arrays rather than logic. As a result, while putting logic into memory chips remains technically elegant, the lack of software support has made this approach non-viable commercially.

However, with technologies such as C-HBM4E and Marvell’s approach to custom HBM4E memory that leverage industry standards and economies of scale, the chances that near-memory or even in-memory processing can be done right are higher than before, according to Schuette.

“What you need is a memory accelerator that generates custom metadata that can be extracted by the same accelerator—in other words, assigning a structure to unstructured data and storing those structures along with the data so that you search for the structure instead of the data,” Schuette said. “It does require a bit of processing, but it doesn’t have to be done on demand but could theoretically be performed in the background during idle times.”

For example, big hyperscalers such as Google or Meta could insert custom in-memory or near-memory accelerators into memory subsystems of their in-house AI accelerators to further optimize their hardware and software stack.

“That is what they should be doing,” Schuette said. “But you cannot just throw things into the HBM [with an accelerator] and then expect the accelerator to magically unravel the Gordian knot. You need to figure out what the data is, what features you have, and make it [usable].”

data.title: At MWC, Ericsson Details AI-Native 6G Timeline
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

BARCELONA, Spain — At the Mobile World Congress (MWC) 2026, Ericsson shared its plans for the telecom industry, highlighting progress on 6G standards, the integration of AI into networks, and the introduction of new hardware.

As the industry anticipates the commercial rollout of next-generation networks later this decade, the Stockholm-based infrastructure manufacturer is positioning its computing architecture to handle shifting data traffic patterns.

Path to 6G standardization

The formal definition of 6G technology is currently under scrutiny within the 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP), the main body that develops international standards and protocols for mobile networks.

The process has moved beyond preliminary discussions into targeted technical specifications. In an interview with EE Times at MWC 2026, Marie Hogan, head of 6G portfolio strategy at Ericsson, outlined the progress made by the working groups. “Now we have had a few 3GPP standardization meetings,” Hogan stated. “In the [radio access network] RAN, we’ve had probably four meetings at this stage. So there have been some decisions already on some very low-level physical layer.”

Early decisions include using orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) waveforms for 6G mobile broadband, but many technical details remain to be worked out. Hardware development will depend on these regulatory frameworks.

“In the middle of this year, we would expect to have some of the more critical physical layer decisions,” Hogan noted, adding that such milestones are necessary for chipset providers and infrastructure developers to plan their manufacturing strategies accurately. While standardizations are progressing, the complete architecture remains years away. “The specification is not fully ready until 2029,” Hogan said.

The upcoming standard will also formally integrate non-terrestrial networks, establishing a harmonized framework for satellite communications. “There’s clearly going to be a discussion on non-terrestrial in 6G,” Hogan stated. “A lot of the good things from the 5G NTN will be inherited into 6G. There will be a bit of work on having smoother mobility between terrestrial and non-terrestrial.”

AI-native network and distributed compute

A central theme of Ericsson’s 2026 announcements is the distinction between retrofitting existing networks with AI and designing a natively intelligent system from the ground up. “In 5G, we’re adding AI capabilities to the network. [In] 6G, we’re defining now, and that has AI as a fundamental principle,” Hogan explained.

This change means central processing units and neural network accelerators will be spread across the network to handle AI tasks. Rather than sending all data to central data centers, Ericsson suggests placing computing power closer to where data is generated, such as at base stations. This reduces response times for applications that need quick results. Tasks that are less urgent or require more computing power can still be sent to central locations. The goal is to balance efficiency, cost, and performance for different uses.

“It’s going to be a hybrid scenario,” Hogan stated. “Some models that require massive compute will likely be centralized if they don’t have strict timing pressure. Smaller models and compute may be localized and integrated into radio or base station hardware. We don’t think heavy compute nodes are needed everywhere; it will likely be a mix of both approaches.”

To support AI in the network, Ericsson introduced AI-ready radios with its own silicon chips. These radios use programmable matrix cores in the Ericsson Many-Core Architecture (EMCA), acting as neural network accelerators for on-site AI tasks. The new hardware is designed to save energy while handling complex AI tasks such as beamforming and spatial computing.

Ecosystem partnerships and migration strategy

Building 6G will require companies to work together so their hardware can operate smoothly. At MWC 2026, Ericsson showed working testbeds with leading semiconductor and consumer electronics companies.

“We have Apple, we have MediaTek, and we have Qualcomm all on our booth, collaborating with us on our 6G test beds,” Hogan said. “We have an ecosystem starting to move toward 6G.”

Additionally, network operators will need to plan their spending carefully as they move to 6G. Many are still paying off investments in 5G non-standalone systems. Ericsson recommends seeing 5G standalone as the key step toward future upgrades.

“The best migration path to 6G is to move to 5G standalone and then move to 6G because what most of the industry is working toward in 3GPP is that we would reuse a large part of the 5G standalone core network,” Hogan stated.

Furthermore, with a 5G standalone core, carriers can try out network slicing and monetization models, providing a baseline to avoid an expensive infrastructure overhaul. Hogan reassured operators, “It’s not in any way a rip and replace. 6G has many capabilities, but there’s no need to rush to implement them all immediately.”

In the software partnership space, Ericsson announced a collaboration with Amazon Web Services (AWS) to launch an Agentic rApp as a Service. Hosted on the AWS cloud infrastructure, the service provides telcos with generative AI tools to execute network optimization workflows via natural language commands, connecting through the standardized R1 interface. The platform is currently undergoing field testing with operators, including Vivo Brazil.

Uplink traffic projections and integrated sensing

The rise of agentic AI, smart glasses, and connected vehicles is changing how networks perform. In the past, most network activity was downloading content, but now devices need to send more data back to large language models, which puts pressure on the network’s uplink. Ericsson’s consumer research, based on interviews with 42,000 people in 27 markets, highlights this trend.

“We expect at least three times more uplink traffic by 2030 and then beyond 2030, probably another three times, and that’s maybe a conservative estimate,” Hogan said. Furthermore, carriers will need to guarantee service outside of dense urban centers. “The other thing we expect from the network point of view is that we need to scale these services to a wider area. There’s an expectation to be able to use these outdoors and traveling, and not just indoors.”

Another new feature in 6G is integrated sensing and communications (ISACs). This lets operators use radio waves to detect physical objects, turning the telecom network into a kind of radar.

“How can you use the network infrastructure for communication as we do today… but also use it for sensing?” Hogan asked. This capability is currently in the trial phase, with potential applications ranging from drone tracking to traffic monitoring. “Sensing can be used for a lot of things on the ground, detecting traffic or even an object [such as] a tree falling down and blocking a line of sight,” Hogan explained.

Geopolitical dynamics in deployment

As 6G technology takes shape, national policies and regional rules are affecting how quickly it will be rolled out. Ericsson expects different countries to adopt 6G at different speeds, depending on their government priorities.

“We can see clearly that the American government and policies are very much focused on making sure they’re leading in 6G,” Hogan stated. In contrast, the European market faces distinct structural hurdles. “In Europe, I think it’s challenging with the type of market we have. You have smaller populations per country, and then you have three or four operators per country… and then there are regulation challenges as well,” she noted.

Furthermore, other markets in Asia are aggressively pursuing intellectual property and deployment leadership. “In China, they’ve done a large push on 5G, 5G standalone, and 5G advanced, and they can move very quickly when they see the need,” Hogan observed. She highlighted explicit governmental targets in other nations: “In India, the government stated they want to have 10% of all of the 6G patents. The Korean government, as usual, is pushing for technology leadership.”

Ericsson’s 6G roadmap focuses on AI-native networks, distributed compute, integrated sensing, and global partnerships, targeting full standardization by 2029 via a 5G standalone transition.

See also:

At MWC, Intel Outlines CPU-Driven Case for Telecom Networks

Telcos Stalling on 5G-SA Waiting for 6G

China Sharpens Strategy in the Global 6G Race

6G Connectivity of Intelligence and Integration

data.title: Taiwan Extends Its Reach from Advanced Chips to Embedded Systems
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

In the global context of embedded computing, real-time performance, security, and energy efficiency can no longer be considered separate requirements. They are, in fact, closely interdependent factors in the design of an advanced embedded system. New architectural choices must therefore consider simultaneous constraints that are often conflicting but which affect the overall reliability of end applications.

While the global embedded security market is growing rapidly, thanks to the native integration of secure boot functions, hardware encryption, application isolation, and protections against physical tampering, at the same time, energy efficiency and power reduction take on a central role. To understand the change in the semiconductor industry, we need to consider that, because of the shift in demand from the cloud to the edge device, embedded computing is evolving from a simple control tool to a platform for distributed intelligence.

For this reason, the use of AI in the dynamic optimization of computing resources, energy management, and workloads is one of the main drivers of development in next-generation embedded systems, particularly for distributed peripheral computing architectures.

Another point to analyze is that, while Moore’s Law remains a valid theoretical reference, productivity has now moved in new directions, and with it the flow of manufacturing. Reducing the physical size alone is no longer enough, which is why a technological leap is needed that encompasses transistor density, advanced process nodes, performance, and consumption—a change that can be achieved only through the adoption of advanced architectures.

In addition, chip design and packaging, which support most of the production costs, are closely interconnected, especially when trying to improve computing performance. The modular organization of components—for example, in chiplet form—also becomes a tool for achieving efficiency and design flexibility, resulting in reduced time to market and total cost of manufacturing.

Semiconductor design is no longer focused on the technology node alone but on integration at the package level. Taiwan addresses all of these technical complexities by adopting an integrated end-to-end approach, extending from the silicon manufacturing process to the integration of hardware, firmware, and application software.

Taiwan at the heart of global embedded computing

Taiwan dominates the global semiconductor market. The island is now the world’s leading hub for contract manufacturing, with foundries capable of creating industrial system-on-chips (SoCs), microcontrollers (MCUs), microprocessors (MPUs), and accelerators dedicated to artificial intelligence on advanced technological nodes.

Taiwan concentrates a significant share of wafer production globally, particularly in the pure-play foundry model, where it holds structural leadership.

Its centrality, however, is not only attributable to the large production capacity, or the diversification of products and markets served, but above all to the complete coverage of the entire supply chain and the high degree of vertical integration between silicon production, specialized processes, advanced packaging, testing, and supply of materials, thanks to a unique ecosystem of fabless IP vendors and companies specialized in packaging and testing.

Close collaboration between chipmakers, system integrators, and end customers accelerates innovation and development time, benefiting economies of scale. In addition, the presence of consolidated expertise in managing large production volumes strengthens the resilience of the supply chain, which makes Taiwan a reference point for the global electronics industry.

Taiwan’s capabilities extend to both advanced technology nodes, used for high-performance computing (HPC) applications, as well as mature, specialized nodes, which are critical for the embedded, automotive, networking, edge AI, industrial, and power electronics industries. The country is a world leader in ODMs and OEMs of embedded systems, single-board computers, computer-on-modules (COM Express, SMARC, Qseven), IoT gateways, and ready-to-use AI edge platforms.

According to Yole Group’s analysis, Taiwan holds roughly a 65% market share in the global semiconductor foundry industry and owns almost a monopoly in the advanced node at 3 nm and below. A high percentage supports the rapid and direct transfer of process innovations to real-time and low-power solutions, requirements considered fundamental in embedded computing.

The modern electronics industry is driven by the need for energy-efficient computing and the progressive migration of computing capabilities from the cloud to the edge. Added to this is the rapid spread of connected embedded systems in which AI, networking, and control must coexist on a single platform.

At the same time, Taiwan plays a central role in the development of intelligent cyber-physical systems that aim to optimize deterministic latency, energy efficiency, functional reliability, and hardware-software security—design constraints as important as computational power. The ability to integrate silicon with firmware and application platforms makes Taiwan a key player in the transition to increasingly autonomous, resilient, and intelligent embedded architectures.

Intelligent power management: the benchmark of embedded AI

Power electronics are a component that is often not very visible but structurally critical for contemporary embedded computing. While the exponential growth of AI is increasing the demand for HPC, both in the data center and at the edge, there is also a need to reckon with power consumption and thermal dissipation management.

For this reason, the ability to intelligently manage energy has become critical. Think, for example, of the consumption of an AI accelerator’s individual units as the level of integration increases.

With the aim of responding to these needs, Taiwan has gained expertise in the integration of power electronics, real-time MCUs, and AI capabilities. The introduction of AI in power management systems helps to dynamically optimize voltages, currents, and load profiles according to real operating conditions, which translates into a reduction in losses, improved system stability, and greater overall resilience.

Innovative chips made in Taiwan show advances in real-time performance, thanks to more predictable multicore architectures, dedicated control computing units, and optimized interconnects that reduce latency. Energy management is improved through more advanced production processes, selective shutdown techniques for unused blocks, and intelligent voltage and frequency management, with benefits in power consumption and dissipation.

For embedded edge systems, which are often deployed in remote or hard-to-access environments, the ability to manage intelligent energy enables greater operational autonomy and helps reduce the total cost of ownership, resulting in greater sustainability of digital infrastructures.

TSMC’s role in semiconductor manufacturing

In addition to being the world’s largest contract chip manufacturer, TSMC is the technology benchmark for Taiwan’s entire embedded ecosystem. Although the Taiwanese technology giant, which also counts Nvidia and Apple among its customers, is often associated with process nodes intended for HPC and mobile computing, its most significant contribution lies in the ability to guarantee production technologies characterized by high reliability, with predictability of electrical parameters and long-term support, essential requirements in industrial applications, automotive, and infrastructure where product lifecycles frequently exceed a decade.

TSMC has recently started mass production of 2-nm semiconductor chips, which represent the most cutting-edge process nodes in terms of density advancements, performance, and power consumption compared with previous models and are also structured differently to accommodate a larger number of components. The new technology will help increase the speed of laptops, reduce the carbon footprint of data centers, and improve object-recognition capabilities for self-driving cars.

The most advanced technological nodes are usually used for the creation of industrial MCUs, automotive SoCs, and ASICs dedicated to intelligent power electronics. Among the TSMC process nodes typically used in the semiconductor industry, there are also 3 nm (the previous generation adopted in high-end products such as FinFET transistors), 5 nm (widely used for smartphones and HPC SoCs), 7 nm, and 6 nm, which is an optimized variant of 7 nm with better power, performance, and area. TSMC’s portfolio also includes 10 nm, 12–16 nm for mid-range, 20–22 nm for embedded applications, and 28 nm for automotive and consumer devices.

From a performance point of view, the 2-nm chips introduce significant improvements over previous nodes. Compared with 3 nm, they offer a reduction in consumption of 25% to 36% for the same performance or an increase in performance of 10% to 15% for the same power. Transistor density increases by 15% to 20%, resulting in improved efficiency per area.

According to TSMC, over the course of about a decade, moving from 7 nm to the next, extremely advanced technologies (such as 1.4 nm in the future), overall energy efficiency will improve up to about 4.2×, with important advances with each generation.

We must also consider the evolution of packaging technologies, which enables the heterogeneous integration of general-purpose CPUs, AI accelerators, high-bandwidth memories, and power modules within a single system. By integrating multiple components into a single module, you can overcome limitations in chip interconnection and enable 2.5D/3D configurations with higher performance than traditional solutions.

One of TSMC’s main technologies includes integrated fan-out (InFO) and chip-on-wafer-on-substrate (CoWoS). InFO eliminates the traditional silicon interposer, replacing it with highly interconnect polymer film materials, reducing cost and package thickness, to achieve high connection density in high-volume mobile and HPC applications. CoWoS, on the other hand, is a 2.5D solution that employs a silicon interposer to connect multiple high-bandwidth, high-performance dies and memories, widely adopted in AI chips and high-end servers.

System-on-integrated-circuit packaging relies on 3D vertical stacking of dies to maximize performance and efficiency, particularly in applications that require high density. TSMC’s 3DFabric advanced packaging platform combines 3D stacking technologies and advanced interconnects for vertical integration of multiple chips and enables the implementation of energy-efficient computing through the combination of CPUs, AI accelerators, memory, and power modules into a single, optimized package. For semiconductors intended for performance applications, this results in a marked improvement in performance, integration density, energy efficiency, and design flexibility.

MPUs and embedded SoCs: the evolution of MediaTek’s role

MediaTek embodies Taiwan’s transition from a silicon manufacturer to a designer of complete embedded platforms. Historically focused on consumer electronics, the company has expanded its portfolio to include industrial applications, advanced IoT, and edge networking, as well as medical systems.

MediaTek’s embedded SoCs combine multicore CPUs, digital-signal processors, neural processing units, and power management subsystems into heterogeneous workload architectures. To meet this growing demand, MediaTek delivers highly integrated platforms, which reduce system complexity and power consumption. From the point of view of MCUs and MPUs, the focus is on the integration of hardware security features (secure boot, trust zones, and cryptographic accelerators) and support for RTOS and Linux in real time.

Advantech and embedded AI platforms for industry

Advantech Co. Ltd. has a strategic role in transforming silicon into complete embedded systems that are ready for adoption in real-world industrial settings. Its hardware and software platforms integrate MCUs, MPUs, and AI accelerators within architectures designed for both edge computing and real-time control and local data processing. In this way, the company positions itself as an advanced system integrator, capable of effectively bridging the gap between semiconductor suppliers and the operational needs of field applications.

Advantech’s approach is strongly oriented toward modularity and scalability to meet the needs of distributed or mission-critical environments, thanks to platforms that support local AI inference, standardized industrial connectivity, and advanced energy management capabilities. Particular attention is paid to the typical requirements of industrial embedded, such as temporal determinism, support for RTOS and real-time Linux distributions, and end-to-end security mechanisms that cover hardware, firmware, and application software together.

Thanks to vertical integration, Advantech solutions are particularly suitable for the development of complex cyber-physical systems, in which AI, automation, and control must operate in a coordinated way.

Arbor: a leader in high-performance embedded integration

Arbor Technology Corp. stands out in the embedded systems industry for its ability to integrate high-performance platforms into compact form factors designed to operate in harsh industrial environments and close to data sources. Its solutions combine multiprocessor processing units, graphics processing components, and specialized accelerators, with a focus on heat dissipation and power consumption.

According to market analysis, within the next few years, energy efficiency and computing density will become decisive criteria in the choice of peripheral computing platforms. Arbor meets the demand through optimized architectures and advanced energy management techniques. Examples include dynamic voltage and frequency regulation and predictive thermal control, which guarantee operational stability even under varying load conditions.

From a design point of view, Arbor platforms make it possible to balance computationally intensive data analysis activities with stringent deterministic time control requirements. This makes them particularly suitable for advanced applications (machine vision, intelligent transport systems, distributed robotics, etc.), in which reliability, operational continuity, and predictability of system behavior are considered essential requirements as much as computing performance.

Specialized AI accelerators and global collaborations

The complexity of AI workloads has necessitated specialized compute components for processing close to data sources. EdgeCortix Inc., a Japanese company, uses the Taiwanese industrial ecosystem and TSMC’s manufacturing capacity to develop dedicated co-processors, specifically designed for real-time inference and maximum efficiency. In addition to increasing performance, the goal is to ensure a sustainable balance between processing capacity, power consumption, and time predictability.

Partnering with TSMC enables EdgeCortix to adopt advanced process technologies and encapsulation solutions, which can reduce processing latency and overall power consumption.

The Taiwanese ecosystem, thanks to the quality and resilience of the supply chain, operates on a wide application spectrum that includes smart grids, electric vehicles, automation, and distributed edge infrastructures.

At the center of it all is its industrial model. The combination of technological continuity, advanced production capacity, intelligent energy management and integration of edge systems create a technological hub that is difficult to replicate by other manufacturing hubs, which allows it to serve both high-performance applications and demanding markets that require long lifecycles and stability over time.

In an era where distributed AI is moving toward devices, Taiwan, in addition to providing components, creates the technological prerequisites for the future of intelligent embedded computing through the enabling of new and increasingly efficient and specialized architectures.

Despite the complex and uncertain geopolitical context on the global chessboard, Taiwan maintains its technological self-sufficiency and confirms itself as the leader in the production of advanced chips.
